g.data-narration: Welcome to Fundamentals of Algorithmic Problem Solving - the cornerstone of computer science and software engineering.
g.text: university - Analysis and Design of Algorithms

g.data-narration: An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined instructions that transforms input into output with five essential properties.
Efficient algorithms power modern technology - from search engines to social networks, determining software performance and scalability.
The first critical step is understanding the problem - identify inputs, outputs, and constraints to design an effective solution.
Algorithm specification requires expressing solutions through pseudo-code with proper structure, control flow, and documentation.
Four major design paradigms guide algorithm development: brute force, divide and conquer, greedy, and dynamic programming approaches.
Algorithm verification uses testing to check outputs and mathematical proof for correctness guarantees on all inputs.
Loop invariants prove correctness by maintaining a true condition before and after each iteration, ensuring valid output at termination.
Time complexity measures execution time growth with input size; space complexity measures memory requirements using Big-O notation.
Complexity classes range from O(1) constant to O(n!) factorial, determining which algorithms scale for large inputs.
The find-maximum algorithm iterates through array comparing each element, achieving O(n) time and O(1) space complexity.
g.text: max = A[1]; for i=2 to n

g.data-narration: The recursive factorial algorithm uses base case and recursive case, making n calls with O(n) time and O(n) space complexity.
Three fundamental strategies: incremental builds solutions step-by-step, recursion solves via smaller instances, backtracking explores all possibilities.
Key takeaways: understand problem inputs/outputs/constraints, choose appropriate design paradigm, prove correctness, analyze complexity.
Exam tips: identify inputs/outputs/constraints first, state complexity in Big-O, use loop invariants, handle edge cases carefully.
g.text: X Using best instead of worst-case
